perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Should Buyer present an offer to purchase the Business, Buyer will provide a financial statement and a personal and business history, and Buyer authorizes Broker and Seller to obtain through standard reporting agencies, financial and cred it information about Buyer and/or the companies Buyer represents. In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.explanation: The attorneys' fees clause is moved from the 'Enforcement' section to the 'Provide Evidence of Financial Ability' section, obscuring its direct relationship to enforcement of the agreement. This makes it harder for a party to quickly find and understand their right to recover fees in case of litigation, weakening enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5(a)(10) allows for the recovery of attorney's fees when authorized by contract, statute, or law. Placing the attorney's fees provision in an unrelated section can make it less obvious and thus harder to enforce, as it is not prominently displayed where one would expect such an important enforcement-related clause to be. Relocating this clause obscures it, making it less discoverable and potentially leading a court to question whether it was knowingly and voluntarily agreed to.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw introduces a legal contradiction. By relocating the attorney's fees clause, the changed text obscures a party's right to recover fees in litigation, weakening enforceability as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation suggests this makes it harder to enforce, as it's not prominently displayed where expected, potentially leading a court to question its knowing agreement. CCP 1033.5(a) allows attorney's fees as costs, and hiding the clause affects its discoverability, impacting the ability to claim these fees, creating a structural flaw. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of Broker providing Confidential Information to Buyer, Buyer agrees to the following: KEEP INFORMATION CONFIDENTIAL Buyer acknowledges that any Confidential Information disclosed to others may be damaging to the Business and the Seller. Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public. Buyer agree s not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement . Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction . Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates. DIRECT ALL CONTACT THROUGH BROKER Buyer shall not contact the Seller or any other individual or entity associated with Seller or the Business including, without limitation , landlords, employees, suppliers or customers except upon the prior written consent of Seller .
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of Broker providing Confidential Information to Buyer, Buyer agrees to the following: DIRECT ALL CONTACT THROUGH BROKER Buyer shall not contact the Seller or any other individual or entity associated with Seller or the Business including, without limitation , landlords, employees, suppliers or customers except upon the prior written consent of Seller . KEEP INFORMATION CONFIDENTIAL Buyer acknowledges that any Confidential Information disclosed to others may be damaging to the Business and the Seller. Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public. Buyer agree s not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement . Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction . Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: By swapping the order of "KEEP INFORMATION CONFIDENTIAL" and "DIRECT ALL CONTACT THROUGH BROKER" section. The direct contact clause is now at the top. Making it seem as the main focus, making the disclosure secondary. This change could lead to arguments about the parties' intent, weakening the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1636 states that a contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. Placing the 'Direct All Contact' clause before the 'Keep Information Confidential' clause obscures the primary intent of the NDA which is to protect confidential information. The relocation can create ambiguity about the key purpose of the agreement, making it harder to prove that the parties mutually intended to prioritize strict confidentiality over direct contact restrictions.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reorders the clauses, placing the 'Direct All Contact' clause before the 'Keep Information Confidential' clause. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1636, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, because it obscures the primary intent of the agreement, which should be to protect confidential information. According to the scraped snippet, 'A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful.' The reordering creates ambiguity about the key purpose, making it harder to prove the parties mutually intended to prioritize strict confidentiality. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' because the organization of the contract affects its clarity and enforceability, and a required legal disclosure (confidentiality) is placed in a less prominent position, potentially misleading parties about its importance.
